answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: "The three sector-specific scenarios presented in this report align with NZ CS 1, and were agreed as follows: Scenario 1: Orderly Transition (Paris Agreement-aligned transition scenario). Scenario 2: Hot House World (high-warming scenario). Scenario 3: Disorderly Transition (additional scenario)."
answers.location_hint: Final-telecommunications-sector-climate-scenarios-July-2024.pdf, p.5; also Climate-related Disclosures Report.pdf, p.6
answers.notes: Spark's Climate-related Disclosures Report labels them as Scenario #1: Orderly transition, Scenario #2: Disorderly transition, Scenario #3: Hothouse — same three scenarios, slightly different ordering/naming convention.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: (1) Orderly Transition: 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels by 2050, declining to 1.4°C by 2100; (2) Disorderly Transition: 2°C by 2050, rising to 2.7°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100; (3) Hot House World: surpassing 2°C by 2050, rising to 3.6°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100.
answers.evidence: "Annual average temperatures increase to 2°C by 2050 (above pre-industrial levels). Then rapidly declines to 1.4°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100. [Orderly Transition] ... Annual average temperatures surpass 2°C by 2050 (above pre-industrial levels). Then increase to 3.6°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100. [Hot House World] ... Annual average temperatures increase to 2°C by 2050 (above pre-industrial levels). Then increases to 2.7°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100. [Disorderly Transition]"
answers.location_hint: Final-telecommunications-sector-climate-scenarios-July-2024.pdf, p.45 (driver table En3); also p.5 and p.19
answers.notes: The Orderly Transition scenario includes temperature overshoot — temperatures rise to 2°C by 2050 then decline to 1.4°C by 2100 due to net-zero action. Spark's CRD report summarises Hothouse as ">3°C" global temperature change.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are explicitly named: (1) IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) — for SSP scenarios; (2) NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research) — for RCP downscaled projections; (3) New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) — for decarbonisation pathways (Tailwinds, Headwinds, Current policy). The sector scenarios were developed by Tonkin + Taylor (T+T) on behalf of the NZ Telecommunications Forum (TCF).
answers.evidence: "each scenario drawing from a range of widely used global and local scenario archetypes, for example the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), the National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA) representative concentration pathways (RCP) and the New Zealand Climate Change Commission decarbonisation pathways."
answers.notes: The sector-level scenario narratives were compiled by Tonkin & Taylor Ltd (T+T), facilitated under the TCF. The underlying scientific scenario data comes from IPCC, NIWA, and the NZ Climate Change Commission.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "Table 4.2: International and national scenario alignment ... Global climate and socio-economic parameters: SSP1-1.9 (IPCC) / SSP3-7.0 (IPCC) / SSP2-4.5 (IPCC) ... NZ specific climate parameters: RCP 2.6 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 8.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 4.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting)"
answers.notes: The document explicitly pairs SSP identifiers (from IPCC AR6) with RCP identifiers (from NIWA downscaled reporting) for each scenario. These are from different scenario families — SSPs are from IPCC AR6 while RCPs are from an earlier IPCC framework. They are used together to provide global and NZ-specific parameters respectively.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "NZ specific climate parameters: RCP 2.6 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 8.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 4.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting)" and "The objective for this project was to create sectoral climate change scenarios to allow organisations to progress their Climate-related Disclosure reporting, and to allow for consistency across the sector in this reporting." and "Sectoral scenarios are inherently broad and encompass elements and storylines relevant to the range of organisations within the sector they have been produced for."
answers.location_hint: Final-telecommunications-sector-climate-scenarios-July-2024.pdf, p.11, p.19; Climate-related Disclosures Report.pdf, p.5
answers.notes: The scenarios are customised in two ways: (1) NIWA RCPs are explicitly described as "downscaled" for NZ-specific climate parameters; (2) the sector-level narratives were purpose-built for the NZ telecommunications sector, incorporating sector-specific drivers identified through stakeholder workshops, going well beyond off-the-shelf use of global scenarios.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "A smaller 'project management' team and wider 'stakeholder group' were established to support and oversee this work... While the wider stakeholder group consisted of 20+ individuals across the nine organisations involved, their role was to bring their sector expertise to this engagement process and provide the inputs for the scenario development. A shortlist of 19 drivers were identified and mapped across each of the three scenarios during two in-person workshops... The scenarios presented here have been developed based on input from the stakeholder representatives from the nine organisations involved in this project... Stakeholders have been provided the opportunity to review and comment on scenarios."
answers.notes: The scenario development involved participatory workshops with 20+ stakeholders from nine organisations. Stakeholders voted on priority drivers, provided inputs, and reviewed/commented on scenarios. Spark's CRD also notes the scenarios "were designed and agreed in consultation across the sector."

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described against which the three climate scenarios are compared. However, a baseline year of 2024 is referenced for time horizon calculations in Spark's report.
answers.evidence: "Time horizons for scenario analysis ... Year relative to baseline year (2024): 2030 [short], 2040 [medium], 2055+ [long]"
answers.notes: The "baseline year" mentioned is a temporal reference point for time horizons, not a climate baseline scenario. No separate "business-as-usual" or "current policies" baseline scenario is described as a comparator against which the three scenarios are assessed. The Climate Change Commission's "Current policy" pathway is used as a dataset for the Hothouse scenario, but is not framed as a standalone baseline comparator.

answers.question: Model used
answers.answer: No specific climate models or integrated assessment models (IAMs) are named by model name. The documents reference data sources (IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, Ministry for the Environment climate models) but do not name specific models such as CLIMADA, REMIND, MESSAGE, etc.
answers.evidence: "As part of our scenario analysis process we updated our physical risk analysis against climate models newly released by the Ministry for the Environment in 2024. This analyses risk to our infrastructure and assets against the risks of sea level rise/coastal flooding, temperature, wind and rain pattern changes."
answers.notes: The documents refer to "climate models newly released by the Ministry for the Environment in 2024" but do not name the specific models. No IAMs or macroeconomic models are named by name in either document.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "The frequency and intensity of extreme weather events increases markedly over the decade, with repeated and unprecedented events causing significant damage to infrastructure across the telecommunications network in NZ. The frequency of events, and associated response and recovery costs put significant strain on the telecommunications and critical infrastructure sectors..." and "Identified potential impacts include: Increased reactive maintenance costs (labour, fleet, contractors, and parts) ... Increased customer disruptions impact revenue, brand, and reputation ... Increased insurance cost ... Increased costs of new infrastructure builds to meet higher climate resilience standards" and "FY25 financial impact: ~$2m" (for resilience/readiness investments related to weather events)
answers.notes: Both documents explicitly address damage and financial impacts from acute physical risks (extreme weather events, flooding, cyclones). Spark's CRD quantifies a current FY25 financial impact of ~$2m for resilience investments related to weather events, and identifies potential future impacts including increased maintenance costs, insurance costs, and infrastructure rebuild costs.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: global trade and supply chain disruption
answers.evidence: "Table 4.3: Shortlisted Driving Forces ... En1: Change in frequency and severity of extreme events (inc flooding and landslides). En3: Extreme temperatures leading to health and infrastructure impacts. ... Ec5: Changes to the global geopolitical landscape. Ec6: Changes to global trade and supply chain. Ec2: Changes to the cost of living. Ec4: Access to finance. T2: Material shortages ... P1: Legislation changes focusing on emissions reduction ... P6: Legislation on managed retreat/adaptation. P7: Legislation changes to trade policy ... P8: Legislation changes to environment (land use)." and "This analyses risk to our infrastructure and assets against the risks of sea level rise/coastal flooding, temperature, wind and rain pattern changes."
answers.notes: The scenarios use a wide range of physical, socioeconomic, policy, and technology variables as drivers. Physical variables include sea level rise, coastal flooding, wind and rain patterns, and extreme weather events. Socioeconomic/policy variables include geopolitical landscape, supply chains, cost of living, access to finance, and various legislative changes.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "each telecommunications sector world has been referenced to one of the SSPs ... Global climate and socio-economic parameters: SSP1-1.9 (IPCC) / SSP3-7.0 (IPCC) / SSP2-4.5 (IPCC) ... NZ specific climate parameters: RCP 2.6 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 8.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting) / RCP 4.5 (NIWA downscaled reporting)" and "we updated our physical risk analysis against climate models newly released by the Ministry for the Environment in 2024. This analyses risk to our infrastructure and assets against the risks of sea level rise/coastal flooding, temperature, wind and rain pattern changes."
answers.notes: The scenarios use global-level SSP parameters from IPCC alongside NZ-specific (regional/local) RCP parameters from NIWA downscaled reporting. Spark's physical risk analysis also uses NZ Ministry for the Environment climate models applied to specific site locations, representing a local/asset-level scale.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short term: 5 years (to ~2030); Medium term: 15 years (to ~2040); Long term: 30+ years (to ~2055+). Rationales are explicitly provided for all three timeframes.
answers.evidence: "Table 4.1: Time horizons chosen by the telecommunications sector ... Short term: 5 years — Rationale: Aligns with telecommunications organisations emissions reductions targets. Medium term: 15 years — Rationale: Aligned with average life of current assets, and when they are likely to be renewed. Long term: 30+ years — Rationale: Aligned with further materialisation of physical risks, particularly on infrastructure." and from Spark's CRD: "Rationale for selection and link to strategic planning horizons and capital deployment plans: [Short] Aligns with emissions reduction target and with future-facing investment horizon. [Medium] Aligned with typical life of technology assets. [Long] Aligned with further materialisation of physical risks, particularly on infrastructure."
answers.notes: Both documents provide explicit rationales for each time horizon. The sector scenarios document also notes these time horizons "differ from those in other sectors due to the lifespan of telecommunications infrastructure and technology." Spark's CRD notes that for risk analysis a timebound 30-year horizon was used, while 30+ years was used for long-term scenario development.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "These scenarios align to the requirements in NZ CS 1 for entities to analyse three scenarios, with each scenario drawing from a range of widely used global and local scenario archetypes, for example the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), the National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA) representative concentration pathways (RCP) and the New Zealand Climate Change Commission decarbonisation pathways. They are also based on scenario archetypes that are consistent with those used by other infrastructure sectors in New Zealand. The scenarios were designed and agreed in consultation across the sector. Given the broad range of inputs and alignment to recognised scenario archetypes we believe the scenarios capture a broad set of possible outcomes that are relevant and appropriate to assessing the resilience of our business model and strategy to climate-related risks and opportunities."
answers.notes: The justification covers: (1) alignment with NZ CS 1 regulatory requirements; (2) use of widely recognised global and local scenario archetypes (IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, NZ CCC pathways); (3) consistency with other NZ infrastructure sectors; (4) co-design with sector stakeholders; and (5) belief that the scenarios capture a broad set of relevant outcomes. The sector scenarios document also justifies time horizon choices based on asset lifespans and emissions targets.
